summary: Package hijacks global console methods on import to exfiltrate log content to a hardcoded Telegram bot
details: The package's declared main (index.js) executes an IIFE at require() time that reassigns the global console.warn and console.error functions. The replacements POST their first argument to https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/sendMessage with hardcoded chat_ids (-1001161709623 for warn, -1001433099398 for error). Any installer whose code does `require('88q')` will, for the lifetime of that process, silently relay every subsequent console.warn/console.error call — which commonly includes diagnostic data, error traces, tokens, and other sensitive content — to a third-party Telegram channel controlled by the author. The module export is structured as `var co = (function(){...}()); module.exports = co` where the IIFE returns nothing, so the public API is `undefined`; the package exists purely for its side effect on the installer's global console. There is no README, empty description, and no documented functionality matching the package name. A secondary file (t.js) contains the author's own hardcoded Cloudflare credentials but is not reachable from the main entrypoint, so it is author self-harm and not the basis for this decision.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
